description: If something exists, then something exists — no matter what you call it.
goal: (-> (exists x (App P x)) (exists y (App P y)))

guides.text: The goal is $\exists x.\; P(x) \to \exists y.\; P(y)$. This should be trivially true — if something is $P$, then something is $P$, regardless of the variable name. But to prove it, we need `exists-elim`: from the existential claim, we open a [witness|witness], prove the [conclusion|conclusion] under that witness, then [assumption|discharge].
Assume the existential claim: something is $P$.
Introduce a fresh [witness|witness] $y$ with `fix`. This is the arbitrary term we'll work with — 'let $y$ be some thing.'
Assume the [witness|witness] satisfies $P$: $P(y)$. This is the instantiated body of the existential, under the fresh name $y$.
Merge the [witness|witness] [atom|atom] $y$ with the [assumption|assumption] $P(y)$ using `and-intro` so both are in the same [context|context].
Extract $P(y)$ from the merged [context|context]. Now you have $P(y)$ at a step whose context contains both $y$ and $P(y)$.

From $P(y)$, form $\exists y.\; P(y)$ using `exists-intro`. Replace the concrete term $y$ with the binding variable $y$ — same name, but now it's the [bound-variable|bound variable] of the [quantifier|quantifier].

Now `exists-elim` [assumption|discharges] everything. It takes the existential (step 1), the [witness|witness] subproof (step 6), and the witness variable name ($y$). The engine checks that $y$ doesn't leak into the [conclusion|conclusion] and removes the witness [atom|atom] and its instantiated body from the [context|context].
Step 7 gives us $\exists y.\; P(y)$ under the [assumption|assumption] $\exists x.\; P(x)$. Discharge to finish.

unlocks.name: ∃-elim
unlocks.description: From an existential claim, open a witness $y$, prove the conclusion $\psi$ under the assumption $\varphi[y/x]$, and discharge — provided $y$ doesn't escape into $\Delta$ or $\psi$.
